does: This paper analyzes bilinear layers as a more interpretable alternative to standard MLPs, demonstrating that despite being nonlinear functions of their inputs, bilinear layers can be fully expressed using only linear operations and third-order tensors. The author shows that bilinear layers achieve better performance than conventional MLPs while remaining mathematically tractable. By decomposing bilinear transformations into explicit tensor operations, the work makes these layers amenable to formal mathematical analysis and provides a concrete representation that avoids the superposition problem where networks represent more features than neurons.
connects: This work extends research on neural network interpretability and the superposition problem, building on efforts to understand how networks encode multiple features in single neurons. It connects to the mechanistic interpretability literature, particularly the framework of transformer circuits developed for attention-only models, which previously lacked tools for analyzing feedforward components. The paper responds to longstanding challenges in interpreting MLPs with elementwise activations by proposing an architectural alternative with clearer mathematical structure. It also engages with broader efforts in the interpretability community to design architectures that are inherently more transparent while maintaining or improving performance.
enables: By establishing that bilinear layers admit formal mathematical analysis through tensor decomposition, this work opens pathways for more rigorous circuit analysis in neural networks and potentially deeper safety insights. The extension of transformer circuit frameworks to include bilinear layers enables mechanistic understanding of models beyond attention mechanisms. Future work could explore whether bilinear architectures scale effectively to large models and whether their mathematical tractability translates to more reliable interpretability methods. The approach suggests a broader research direction of designing architectures simultaneously optimized for interpretability and performance, potentially influencing architectural choices in safety-critical applications where formal verification is valuable.
